text: A new family of ruthenium(II/III/IV)–pentalene complexes The equation [Ru(n)(tda)(py)2](m+) = complex; n number of water molecules; m charge is fulfilled by presenting a new family of complexes with the general formula [Ru(n)(tda)(py)2](m+) (n = 2, m = 0, 1; n = 3, m = 1, 2(+); n = 4, m = 2, 3(2+))) that consists of tda(2-) with the formula [2,2′:6′,2″-terpyridine]-6,6″-dicarboxylate ligand, such as the complex 4H(+) (4-Hypers), [Ru(IV)(OH)(tda-κ-N3O)(py)2](+)), which proved to be an excellent water oxidation catalyst, generated via hydroxo coordination to 3(2+) under basic conditions. The complexes were synthesised, isolated and fully characterised by a set of analytical, UV-Vis, 1D and 2D NMR, EPR and computational methods, along with a full electrochemical analysis using CV, DPV and coulometry. X-ray solid state data at room temperature was recorded for each monocrystal. In the oxidation state IV, the ruthenium centre is seven-coordinate and diamagnetic, whilst in the oxidation state II the complex has a dangling, unbonded carboxylate and six-coordinate ruthenium, both being also diamagnetic. In the oxidation state III, the coordination number is intermediate between those for II and IV states. Additional information on the complexes generated in-situ were obtained using the same set of analytical methods together with protonation of different charges and also complexes generated under different oxidation states. Complex 4H(+) could be generated potentiometrically as well as voltammetrically from 3(2+)), and both complexes seem to be stabilised and coexist in solution. Whilst 3(2+)) is catalytically inactive towards water oxidation, the complex 4H(+) was proven to be an excellent catalytic material following the analysis of the foot of the wave and yielding one of the highest turnover frequency recorded so far; 8000 s(-1) at pH 7.0 and 50 50 000 s(-1) at pH 10.0, via density functional theory calculations performed on the species involved along the catalytic cycle for complex 4H(+)) and showing that these species combined give a great understanding of the catalytic process and of the important role of the dangling carboxylate in facilitating the formation of the O-O bond and the related catalytic cycle activation energies.
